transcript.text: Hey, this is my favorite animal requested by Bhakti Omsat.
That's a good video to do.
My favorite animal is the cat, the house cat.
I love house cats.
As soon as I get me a place to live, I'd get me a house cat.
I don't think it'd be right to have a cat that I live in in my van.
He'd get bored running around in the hot car.
It's like, if I have to leave a place, how am I going to find my cat?
Maybe I could give it a try, but no.
I'll get a hamster.
I guess that's my second favorite animal.
Maybe I'll get a hamster to run around in my car.
Free, running wild.
We'll see about that in the summertime.
But anyways, yeah, house cat's my favorite.
Because they're just like stuffed animals, you know?
They're so soft, and they smell good all the time.
And they're clean, and they love it when you scratch them.
They're like, ooh.
And they love to, like, you know, paw at you.
I had a little kitty.
Man, I should have brought her back to America.
Named Candy.
In Chile.
And she was the coolest cat.
She'd, like, talk to me.
She'd run in the room and be like, meow.
And I'd be like, meow.
She'd be like, meow, meow.
And I'd be like, meow, meow.
And I'd go like, meow, meow, meow.
And she'd go, meow, meow, meow, meow, meow.
So every time I'd go, meow, she goes, meow.
And we'd do it back and forth.
And then she'd come and bury her, like, nose in my forearm.
Like, as deep as she could.
I mean, my armpit.
And then I, like, scratch her all the time.
And she'd just, like, run right up to me.
And, you know, I'd get all, I'd squeeze the ticks off her belly.
Because I guess there's lots of ticks down there.
And also, we had another cat named Belen.
And, you know, cats, different cats have totally different personalities, you know.
I think they've got plenty of personalities like dogs.
Belen was totally mellow.
Belen was, like, what was totally cool.
Like, it totally let you, like, pick her up and grab her.
And it was really mellow.
But, like, it was, like, always gone.
I guess in the beginning.
And then Candy was always gone.
It was the one that just left and was gone all the time.
Like, it's exploring.
And Belen was there.
Belen was this cat that we adopted.
Because, like, I was in front of my house.
And, like, right on the roof, there was this, I heard this cat going, meow, meow.
And I could tell she was, like, me, help me, save me.
So, I was, like, oh.
So, I just went up there and grabbed her.
Brought her down and fed her.
And then she was our cat.
And another time, there's this tiny little kitty.
It was, like, this big that was, like, meow, save me, in front of the house.
And I went to bring it in.
But my wife was, like, no, you get that out of it.
Put her on the street.
I felt so bad about that.
Hopefully, somebody else picked him up.
Yeah, I saw a couple of litters pop out from this cat from around the corner.
Yeah, I got my two cats fixed.
We had another cat named Gray.
Plomo.
Plomy.
But, uh, he even disappeared.
I guess he died from poisoning.
That was too bad.
He was a cute little kitty.
Plomy.
But, um, we had a cat I've grown up to named Luis.
He died when I was, like, 16 or something.
He's older than me.
And this, like, wild cat that we didn't even bring when we moved.
I guess my parents didn't like it.
And we had her for, like, a year.
My name was Fernie.
She was, like, running.
It was kind of, like, wild.
Maybe she was part wild.
Because my mom has these two cats now.
Or it's just one cat.
Which is a, um, um, Bengal cat.
Which is, like, ninth generation, um, Australian leopard cat.
Which is a wild, house-sized cat.
House cat-sized wild cat in, like, Southeast Asia.
And so they bred him with house cats.
And so, yeah, nine generations later, it's the, uh, Bengal.
And they're really, they're different personality-wise.
They're, like, fascinated by water.
They'll steer at the water.
Like my mom's cat, you know.
They'll just, like, steer at the water dripping, you know.
And when you take a shower, they peek in.
And, you know, like down the toilet bowl.
So, um, but also, mainly they, um, yeah, you can't pick them up, man.
Like, when they don't know you and they're kittens,
They literally never let you pick you up.
Let you pick them up.
Which, that's totally different from a normal cat.
But now they do.
Now she's normal.
But, um, yeah, but they clean themselves better.
They don't shed.
And, but other than that, they look normal.
I guess their face is maybe a little bit wilder.
I'm just, um, narrow, but I don't know.
Um, yeah, dude, but cats, I love cats.
It's just, like, they're so content with life.
They, you know, they're so happy.
It's just like, yeah, yeah.
And, they're so affectionate.
Always jumping up on you.
Yeah, I love cats.
Sometimes they give me the allergies, but I get used to it, I think.
It gives me, like, the sneezes.
But, I think I get used to that.
Um, so that's my favorite animal.
I love dogs, too.
If I had a big house, I'd have a dog, and I'd go running with them up in the mountains.
But, that'd be cool fun.
But, and hamster, I might get a hamster from my house.

That was cool.

They liked it, and that was their house.
That was pretty cool.
Um, but if they got out, they'd fly away.
They lived in there with my little hamster that ran free through the house.
Those things were smart, man.

Um, because I, I guess I had a big room that was open, and, but, like, I had this big pot that was, like, this high.
And, um, it had, like, I guess, like, a budding tree or something in it that I was trying to grow.
And, um, I guess he could smell the dirt, or he knew there was dirt in there, because hamsters like to dig.
I guess they're little, like, hole-digging rodents, um, instinctually.
So he somehow figured out that if he would shimmy himself up between the wall and my, uh, and my clothing shelves, like a frickin' rock climber,
They could jump up on the top of the, of the, um, drawers and then jump down into, like, this far.
Down into the, uh, the dirt and dig.

I should have left him in there, though, longer, because he was loving it.
But, um, yeah, then he'd run away.

And then I'd, like, I'd leave, and soon, soon enough, he'd shoot out.
And, like, you know, a couple times I saw him, once I saw him running down the road, down my hallway, and I picked him up, he was just, like, running for it as fast as he could.
But the funny thing about hamsters is they can't run very fast.
That's why they make great, um, um, pets, man.
You can always catch him.
Yeah, but once we saw him dead, I think I had that one, like, right on the freaking sidewalk outside.
I don't know how he got out.
Must have gotten out, and then the house cat got him.
Because I had a house cat, too.
